coredata.dc:title: Modeling state-of-charge dependent mechanical response of lithium-ion batteries with volume expansion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gilaki, Mehdi
Sahraei, Elham
coredata.dc:description: The behavior of lithium-ion batteries under mechanical abuse conditions has been studied extensively in recent years. Typically, characterization is performed by conducting experiments on discharged batteries to minimize the chances of thermal runaway and fire. However, studies have shown that material models calibrated at discharged state may not be as accurate at higher states of charge. In this study, a combined experimental-numerical method is proposed to simulate the stiffening of mechanical response in electrode stacks/jellyrolls of lithium-ion batteries at various states of charge and levels of confinement. Our study indicates that the mechanical properties of the jellyroll do not inherently change as a function of the state of charge, rather, the primary cause of the higher stiffness in charged batteries is the volume expansion of the jellyroll during charging. Therefore, mechanical characterization tests are conducted at the discharged state and the expansion of the jellyroll is induced in the finite element models to match the equivalent volume at the desired state of charge. In the second phase of the simulation, mechanical abuse loading is applied to validate the accuracy of simulations using the test data. This method was applied and validated for both pouch and cylindrical batteries with high accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
State-of-charge
Experiments
Finite element modeling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Volume expansion of cylindrical cell at higher SOC
Volume expansion of pouch cell at higher SOC
Simulation results of the cylindrical Cell
Simulation results of the pouch Cell
Applications and limitations of the proposed approach

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CT scan of the discharged battery; measurement of 4 jellyroll revolutions.
Cell transverse compression test, (a) test setup and a deformed cell, (b) force displacement curves for 0 % 50 % and 100 % SOC specimens.
Cell rod indentation test, (a) test setup and a deformed cell, (b) force displacement curves for 0 % and 100 % SOC specimens.
Force-displacement curves obtained from cylindrical punch testing on pouch batteries (Li et al., 2018).
(a) Stress-strain curve used for the jellyroll in the radial direction, (b) flat compression FE model, (c) rod indentation FE model.
Curves used for applying volume expansion: (a) dynamic relaxation phase, (b) transient analysis phase.
(a) The calibrated stress-strain curve for the pouch cell, (b) FE model of cylindrical punch on the pouch cell.
FE models of the pouch battery for different loading scenarios: (a) Case A and B, (b) Case C, (c) Case D.

FE simulation results of cylindrical batteries: (a) radial stress in the jellyroll after volume expansion, (b) Von-Mises stress for the casing after volume expansion (MPa), (c) transverse compression on cylindrical cell, (d) force-displacement comparison for transverse compression, (e) rod indentation on cylindrical cell, (f) force-displacement comparison for rod indentation.
(a) Cylindrical punch simulation on pouch cell, (b) Comparison of force-displacement found from simulation with the experimental data.

FE simulations of the pouch cell: (a) free expansion during charging, (b) contour plot of Z-stress for battery in Case A & B after volume expansion (MPa), (c) plot of Z-stress for Case C after charging, (d) plot of Z-stress for Case D after preloading, (e) plot of Z-stress for Case D after charging, (f) force displacement comparison for cylindrical punch on pouch cell.
Thickness comparison for 4 jellyroll revolutions for a cell at 0% vs. 100 % SOC (measurements are in microns, µm).
Schematics of charging and punch testing configurations studied for the pouch cell.
% Errors based on the displacements at a force of 30 kN for the flat compression and 6 kN for the rod indentation FE models.
% Errors based on the displacements at a force of 30 kN for the cylindrical punch FE models.

head.ce:title: Modeling state-of-charge dependent mechanical response of lithium-ion batteries with volume expansion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electric Vehicle Safety Lab (EVSL), Department of Mechanical Engineering, Temple University, Philadelphia, PA 19122, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electric Vehicle Safety Lab (EVSL), Department of Mechanical Engineering, Temple University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electric Vehicle Safety Lab (EVSL), Department of Mechanical Engineering, Temple University, Philadelphia, PA 19122, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The behavior of lithium-ion batteries under mechanical abuse conditions has been studied extensively in recent years. Typically, characterization is performed by conducting experiments on discharged batteries to minimize the chances of thermal runaway and fire. However, studies have shown that material models calibrated at discharged state may not be as accurate at higher states of charge. In this study, a combined experimental-numerical method is proposed to simulate the stiffening of mechanical response in electrode stacks/jellyrolls of lithium-ion batteries at various states of charge and levels of confinement. Our study indicates that the mechanical properties of the jellyroll do not inherently change as a function of the state of charge, rather, the primary cause of the higher stiffness in charged batteries is the volume expansion of the jellyroll during charging. Therefore, mechanical characterization tests are conducted at the discharged state and the expansion of the jellyroll is induced in the finite element models to match the equivalent volume at the desired state of charge. In the second phase of the simulation, mechanical abuse loading is applied to validate the accuracy of simulations using the test data. This method was applied and validated for both pouch and cylindrical batteries with high accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have many applications in everyday life, from small electronic devices to electric vehicles. Throughout the life of these batteries, in many situations, they may experience mechanical loadings such as crushes or accidental drops. Effects of these abnormal conditions on the safety (Finegan et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019) and electrical properties (Stacy et al., 2020; Derakhshan et al., 2021) of lithium-ion batteries have been studied in the literature. Mechanical characterization typically involves various experiments at the cell (Wierzbicki and Sahraei, 2013; Gilaki and Sahraei, 2019), jellyroll (Gilaki et al.; Avdeev and Gilaki, 2014) and component levels (Gilaki and Sahraei, 2019; Zhang et al., 2016; Bulla et al., 2021, 2020). If the goal of the characterization tests is to calibrate a material model for finite element analysis, several types of experiments are required. The complexity of these tests depends on various aspects such as the battery’s form factor (i.e., pouch (Sahraei et al., 2012, 2015; Dixon et al., 2018), prismatic (Sahraei et al., 2016; Kermani and Sahraei, 2019; Kermani et al., 2019; Kisters et al., 2021) or cylindrical (Wierzbicki and Sahraei, 2013); Greve and Fehrenbach, 2012), the material model used (i.e., isotropic or anisotropic (Gilaki et al.; Xu et al., 2016a; Song et al., 2021), and the level of accuracy desired in the finite element model (i.e., homogenized (Wang et al., 2017) or detailed (Gilaki and Avdeev, 2016)). Performing the necessary experiments can be costly and time consuming. Due to the cost and safety concerns of these tests, in many applications, the characterization experiments are performed on discharged batteries. While the calibrated finite element models based on those experiments may be accurate for discharged states, whether the models are still valid for charged and partially charged batteries is an important question that needs to be answered. If the mechanical behavior of batteries changes significantly after charging, the developed finite element models may not be credible in most real-life applications such as the case when an electric car with a partially charged battery pack is in an accident (Gilaki et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2018). Many studies have attempted to address how state of charge can influence the mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion batteries by looking at changes at the cell level (Xu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2024; Jia et al., 2023) and the electrode and particle levels (Kumar et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021; Duan et al., 2023). Most of these studies focused on how SOC affects the onset of short circuit (stress or strain), thickness changes and the tensile properties of electrodes. When looking at cell-level behaviors, the SOC effects can also be highly dependent on the form factor of the cell being studied, e.g., pouch cells with soft casings versus cylindrical cells with hard metal shells.

Meier et al. (2013) performed an experimental study of the state-of-charge dependence and thermal runaway behavior of large pouch batteries. The results of their hemispherical punch testing showed no difference in the mechanical stiffness of charged versus discharged cells before onset of internal short circuit. The distinction between the cells became evident in their post-failure and thermal runaway behavior. The discharged cells did not go through any thermal events after the short circuit was detected by a drop in voltage. However, the charged cells experienced a significant increase in temperature, ballooning due to the gases generated at onset of short circuit and decomposition of the cell due to the above sequence of events. This occurred regardless of the fact that the force-displacement behavior before reaching short circuit was identical between charged and discharged cells. Changes in the response of cylindrical batteries as a function of SOC are a different matter. Xu et al. (2016b) studied the effects of SOC on mechanical behavior of cylindrical batteries and their components. Their experiments showed that increasing SOC from 0 % to 70 % leads to higher stiffness in cylindrical batteries under transverse compression tests. After disassembling the batteries, they also observed a thickness increase in the anode layers for batteries with higher SOC. The thickness of the cathode layers was not affected significantly by the change in SOC. They also tested the components under compression and concluded that the change in SOC affects the mechanical properties of the components (mainly anode layers). Xu et al. (2016a) used a method analogous to strain-rate effects to model the changes in the strength of the cell at different SOCs. This approach is based on the assumption that the mechanical properties of the jellyroll are inherently dependent on the SOC. Although this approach works for cylindrical batteries, it means that even for pouch batteries that are not confined, changing SOC should lead to different behavior in the battery which is not the case in real-life experiments conducted on pouch cells (Meier et al., 2013; Li et al., 2018). Additionally, some other findings such as pouch test data reported in the literature (Meier et al., 2013) suggest that the small changes in the properties of the anode layers might not have a major effect at the macro scale of the cell. Li et al. (2018) performed experiments on confined and unconfined pouch cells. The authors observed that if the batteries are charged while confined between rigid plates, like cylindrical cells, they exhibit higher stiffness during a flat punch test. However, there was no change in the response of charged and discharged cells if they were not confined. Their findings showed that the change in stiffness observed in the cell level tests was mainly a function of the extent of cell confinement. If a method could implement the change in the behavior of the battery during charge and discharge and be applicable to pouch cells with and without confinement as well as cylindrical batteries, it would be very beneficial and effective.

The hypothesis of the present research is that the change in response of charged cells is directly a result of the cell-level volume expansion due to intercalation. Because of their soft enclosures, swelling in pouch cells can be measured using an accurate displacement measuring device during cycling. Various techniques to measure these swellings are reported in Ref (Zhang and Tang, 2012; Fu et al., 2013; Cannarella et al., 2014). When a lithium-ion battery is cycled, the intercalation of lithium ions into the anode leads to volume changes in the electrode, ultimately increasing the volume at the cell level. If the battery has a soft enclosure and is not confined, as in the case of a pouch cell, the volume change does not cause significant internal stresses. However, for batteries with hard enclosures such as cylindrical and prismatic cells, the volume expansion causes internal stresses which in turn can lead to a stiffer behavior when the battery is subjected to additional mechanical loading such as transverse compression or punch deformation.

In the present study, a new method is proposed to simulate the mechanical response of lithium-ion batteries at a higher state of charge by performing characterization tests at 0 % state of charge. The method includes three steps. The first step is to perform characterization tests and calibrate a material model that properly predicts the mechanical behavior of the jellyroll/electrode stacks at 0 % state of charge. An experimental approach is then utilized to estimate the amount of expansion in the jellyroll/electrode stack during charging. In the third step, the volume expansion is applied to the finite element model to induce prestrains and prestresses in the battery, depending on the extent of confinement present for the cell. Then, the model can undergo the desired mechanical abuse loading scenario for validation purposes and to study the behavior of the cell at higher states of charge. The proposed method was validated for two cell form factors, cylindrical and pouch. The cylindrical cell was tested in two configurations: compression between flat plates and rod indentation. For the pouch cell, the model was validated by simulating a series of cylindrical punch scenarios on constrained and unconstrained batteries. The developed finite element models predicted the behavior of the batteries at various states of charge quite accurately.

In the following sections, first, details about the experiments performed at various SOCs are provided and the amount of jellyroll volume expansion is estimated from the CT scan data. The finite Element modeling approach is introduced in Section 3 and the corresponding results and comparisons with the experiments are discussed in Section 4. Each section is divided into subsections for cylindrical and pouch cells. The last two sections are Discussion and Conclusions, where alternative approaches for volume expansion estimation and modeling methods are summarized and a final conclusion of the research is presented.

The numerical methods proposed and discussed in this study are applied to both cylindrical and pouch batteries. First, a series of experiments was conducted on an 18650 cylindrical battery, to characterize the response of the cell at various states of charge. Then, a finite element model was developed to incorporate the volume expansion effects and used to predict the SOC-dependent mechanical response of the cell. In the next step, a finite element model was developed for a pouch cell, and the prediction of SOC-dependent characteristics of the cell was validated against various experiments reported in the literature.

A set of lithium-iron-phosphate 18650 cylindrical batteries with a nominal capacity of 1.5 Ah and a nominal voltage of 3.2 V was used for the current study. The present authors have reported a series of mechanical and electrical measurements conducted on this type of battery (Gilaki et al.; Sahraei et al., 2019; Soudbakhsh et al., 2020). For this study, cycling of batteries was performed using an eight-channel Maccor 4200 battery tester with a maximum voltage of 20 V and a maximum current of 15 A per channel, during which charge and discharge cutoff voltages were set to 3.65 V and 2.5 V, respectively. Two sets of tests were conducted on cylindrical cells. The first set of experiments aimed to estimate the extent of volume expansion in the jellyroll during charge/discharge cycles. The second set was planned to characterize the SOC-dependent mechanical behavior of the batteries under flat compression and rod indentation.

Being confined within the shell casing, there is no direct way to measure the volume change for cylindrical batteries. Therefore, computed tomography scans of discharged and charged cells were used to measure the in-situ change in the thickness of jellyroll layers. A new cell was used for this purpose. It has been shown in the literature that the amount of expansion in the jellyroll for the first few cycles can be different from the subsequent cycles (Li et al., 2018). Therefore, the battery underwent 5 cycles at 1 C rate before being CT scanned at 0 % SOC. A NorthStar Skyscan 1172 CT scanner was used with a pixel size of 3 microns for maximum precision. The scanning voltage and current were set to 100 kV and 100 µA, respectively, and the scanning time was about 17 hours. To obtain a base measurement for the thickness of the jellyroll layers, a cross section at the mid-length of the jellyroll was chosen, and the thickness of four jellyroll revolutions (including anode, separator, cathode, separator) was measured. The four revolutions were selected far from the casing to allow for higher accuracy in measuring thickness changes (see Fig. 1). The measurement was repeated 8 times around the jellyroll at every 45° angle to minimize measurement errors. The battery was then cycled 5 more times and the CT scan was repeated at 100 % SOC. All the measurements were repeated on the same cross section and the same angles. Table 1 lists the measured jellyroll thicknesses (for the four revolutions shown in Fig. 1) for the cylindrical cell at 0 % and 100 % SOCs. The average thickness for the four revolutions was 1.3875 mm for the fully discharged battery and 1.4289 mm for the fully charged battery. The difference, which is 41.40 μm, can be attributed to the volume expansion during charging. Studies reported in the literature have shown that strain changes linearly as a function of SOC. Therefore, it is assumed that the volume expansion is linear between 0 % and 100 % SOC, and the above two measurements can fully characterize the cell's response at intermediate SOCs as well.

To characterize the effects of state of charge on the mechanical behavior of cylindrical batteries, lateral compression tests were performed. Each of the batteries was cycled 5 times at 1 C rate, and the sixth cycle was stopped at the predefined state of charge before applying the mechanical load. An Instron 5985 load frame equipped with a 250 kN load cell was used for the testing. The tests were conducted between two Instron compression platens (model T1425–1012) which were connected to similar capacity compression rods of the load frame. An LVDT deflection sensor (model 2601–043) was connected to the compression platens to increase the accuracy of displacement measurements. Fig. 2a shows the test setup and one of the cells after deformation. The load frame was set up inside a walk-in fume hood. Before placing the sample in the test setup, load cell and displacement calibrations were performed. After calibrating the load cell, the top platen was moved towards the bottom platen until they barely touched and a slight increase in the force was observed. The displacement was set to zero and the top platen was then moved away until the displacement was exactly equal to the diameter of the cell. The displacement was set to zero again before proceeding with the next steps. The above-mentioned calibration and adjustment procedure improves consistency and repeatability of the tests. The batteries were compressed between two flat plates at a displacement rate of 1 mm/min. The first two samples were tested at 0 % SOC to ensure repeatable results were obtained. The other cells were compressed at 50 % and 100 % SOC. Comparison of the force-displacement curves shows that the battery exhibits a stiffer response as the state of charge increases to 50 % and 100 % (Fig. 2b).

To confirm the behavior of the battery at higher SOC, rod indentation tests were performed as the second test configuration. For this purpose, an indenter fixture was designed, manufactured and attached to the top compression rod of the load frame. A rigid steel plate was connected to the bottom rod to act as support for the battery during the test ( Fig. 3a). The diameter of the indenter was 15.8 mm and the indentation was applied to the mid-length of the jellyroll inside the casing. The voltage of the battery was also measured during the test to monitor the onset of short circuit. More details about the test setup can be found in (Gilaki et al.). This test was conducted on batteries at 0 % SOC and 100 % SOC. Fig. 3a shows the 100 % SOC cell after deformation and Fig. 3b compares the obtained force-displacement curves. Overall, the behavior of the battery under indentation when SOC increases is similar to the case of flat compression test, i.e., the stiffness of the battery increases at higher SOCs. Also, in both cases, the batteries with higher SOC experienced a force drop at smaller displacements. However, unlike the flat compression tests, in the indentation tests, the maximum force of the charged battery was lower compared to discharged cell.

For the pouch cell, the experimental data needed for this study were extracted from some of the tests reported by Li et al. (2018). Their experiments were conducted on a commercial 20 Ah NMC chemistry pouch cell used for electric vehicles. The thickness of the battery was 8 mm. Two series of tests were performed on this cell: volume expansion of unconstrained cell and indentation tests on the cell with and without external constraints. To quantify the volume expansion, they measured the thickness change of the pouch cell during charge and discharge using a 3D camera system and the DIC method. The measured data showed that the volume change during the first cycle was considerably different from the subsequent cycles. After the initial four cycles, the volume change reached a constant value and on average, the cell expanded and contracted by about 0.1184 mm during each charge-discharge cycle.

The second set of tests on the pouch cell involved indentation under external constraint. The battery was constrained between two rigid plates before conducting cylindrical indentation tests to assess whether the presence of the external constraint changes the mechanical behavior of the cell during indentation. Li et al. (2018) designed a special fixture for this purpose, where the battery was constrained between rigid plates and the applied pressure was measured using force sensors. After the battery was constrained, indentation test was conducted using a load frame to compare the load cases with different constraint conditions. Four loading scenarios were considered: in Case A, the battery was charged to 100 % SOC and was indented without the use of the fixture. The battery in Case B was also charged to 100 % SOC outside the fixture. It was then placed inside the fixture with zero pre-tightening pressure before conducting the indentation test. In Case C, the discharged battery was placed between the rigid plates (with zero out-of-plane force), then charged to 100 % SOC and subjected to indentation. The battery in Case D went through a similar procedure, except that there was a pre-tightening force of 5 kN before charging the battery. Table 2 shows a schematic summary of all the four cases. The first row illustrates the level of confinement of the battery during charging, while the second row shows whether the fixture was used during punch loading. As depicted in Fig. 4, the results show that the force-displacement curves for Case A and Case B are similar, indicating that as long as the cycling of the cell is performed without external constraints, the mechanical behavior of the battery is not affected during external loading. However, a comparison between Case B and Case C shows that if the battery is charged between two rigid constraints and the free expansion of the cell is prevented, it exhibits significantly stiffer behavior during indentation. If there is pre-tightening force, as in Case D, the curve shifts further to the left. These results indicate that although previous studies suggest no difference in the mechanical behavior of pouch cells under different SOC conditions, the presence of external constraints can lead to different outcomes. A real-life example of external constraint is battery packs consisting of stacks of pouch cells.

The main idea of the present study is to model the effects of state of charge on the mechanical behavior of lithium-ion batteries by using only mechanical characterization experiments at 0 % SOC. Experimental studies (Li et al., 2018) have revealed that when batteries are not confined, they essentially show similar behavior under mechanical loading regardless of their SOC. This suggests that state of charge does not noticeably change the inherent mechanical properties of the jellyroll. However, the changes in the force-displacement curves for cells with hard shell casings or under confinement come primarily from the prestress generated in the cell due to volume expansion during charge cycles. A universal finite element modeling approach to simulate the SOC dependence of the batteries should be able to simulate the difference in the response of the jellyroll as a function of the level of confinement. The method used in this study is based on an analogy between the volume change due to cycling and thermal expansion. Both phenomena have similar outcomes in the sense that they cause free expansion and no stresses when there are no external constraints. However, when the expansion is prevented, in both cases, internal stresses are developed. Therefore, in this study, to simulate the changes in the response of the cell at various states of charge, a volume expansion is applied to the jellyroll. This will generate prestresses in the model only if the cell is confined and the free expansion is prevented by the confinement.

Material characterization of the cylindrical battery at 0 % SOC has been reported in detail in a previous work by the authors (Gilaki et al.). In that work, an accurate anisotropic material model (modified honeycomb from the LS-DYNA library) was calibrated and validated under various mechanical loading conditions for a fully discharged cell. The same material model is used in the present study, with the addition of a volume expansion step at higher state-of-charge conditions. The jellyroll was modeled using solid elements with an average size of 0.8 mm and element formulation was set to 0. The stress-strain curve calibrated in the jellyroll’s radial direction is plotted in Fig. 5a. For the casing, fully integrated shell elements (element formulation 16) with an element size of 0.7 mm and a thickness of 0.25 mm were used. The material used for the casing was Hill 3 R which is material model 122 in the LS-DYNA library. The jellyroll was constrained within the shell casing using the LS-DYNA contact definition. A rigid wall was defined under the battery and for the flat compression model, a rigid plate applied the compressive displacement from the opposite direction as depicted in Fig. 5b. In the rod indentation model, the rigid plate was replaced by a rigid cylinder with a diameter of 15.8 mm to model the indenter used in the experiments (Fig. 5c). More details on the finite element model of the cylindrical cell at 0 % SOC can be found in the aforementioned publication (Gilaki et al.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Volume expansion of cylindrical cell at higher SOC
ce:section.ce:para:
Using the CT data of charged and discharged batteries, a volume expansion coefficient can be estimated for the jellyroll. The measured thickness of four revolutions of the discharged cell was 1.387 mm and it expanded by 41.40 μm after charging. Coefficient of volume expansion was calculated by analogy to the coefficient of thermal expansion using Eq. (1):
(1)
α =
1
h
∆ h
∆ SOC
Where h is the thickness of the cell, Δh is the thickness change and
∆ SOC
is the change in the state of charge that is assumed to be 1, for a 100 % SOC. By replacing the parameters in the equation, the coefficient of volume expansion is found to be
α =
0.0298. In order to induce this volume expansion, the LS-DYNA keyword *load_thermal_load_curve was used. Application of the volume expansion before structural loading can be performed in two different ways. One method is to perform a multi-step simulation, where the first one initializes the model by activating *interface_springback_lsdyna keyword and applying the volume expansion. Then a subsequent simulation calls the prestresses and deformations from the first simulation and continues to perform the structural analysis on the initialized model. In the second approach, which is used here for the cylindrical cell, the volume expansion is applied during dynamic relaxation at the beginning of the simulation. Two expansion curves were defined. The first one, used during the dynamic relaxation phase (see Fig. 6a), ramps the load to the defined level of state of charge and then maintains it at a constant level until the solution converges. During this phase, a pseudo temperature of 1°C (to reach 100 % SOC) is applied in a short time (1 ms in the example of Fig. 6a) to induce the volume expansion. If the goal is to reach a lower state of charge the pseudo temperature needs to be adjusted accordingly (i.e., 0.5°C for 50 % SOC). The second curve is a constant line to maintain the prestresses during the transient phase of the solution when mechanical loading is applied (Fig. 6b). In LS-DYNA, two curves can be used as inputs for the *load_thermal_load_curve keyword: LCIDDR is the curve defining dynamic relaxation (i.e., Fig. 6a), while LCID is what LS-DYNA uses during the transient phase (i.e., Fig. 6b).

The finite element model of the pouch cell under cylindrical punch loading was also developed in LS-DYNA, using solid elements homogenizing the electrode stack and the battery pouch. Dimensions of the pouch model were 210 mm×130 mm×8 mm and the cell was meshed using 1.5 mm×1.5 mm×1 mm size elements. A rigid wall was under the cell and a rigid cylinder with a diameter of 24 mm was modeled as the punch with shell elements sized 0.5 mm to 1 mm. The crushable foam material model from the LS-DYNA material library and fully integrated elements were used for the battery. The input compression stress-strain was calculated based on the force-displacement curve measured during cylindrical punch tests reported in (Li et al., 2018). The calculated stress-strain curve was also adjusted to account for the deformation around the cylindrical punch as explained in Sahraei et al. (2014). The calibrated stress-strain curve and the finite element model are shown in Fig. 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Volume expansion of pouch cell at higher SOC
ce:section.ce:para:
Similar to the approach used for cylindrical batteries, the first step of the method is to calibrate a volume expansion coefficient for the electrode stack. For pouch batteries, monitoring thickness change during charge and discharge can be used directly to estimate the volume expansion coefficient. Experiments discussed earlier (Li et al., 2018) showed that for the pouch cell under study, there was an average thickness change of 0.1184 mm during charging. Since the nominal thickness of the cell was 8 mm, the coefficient of volume expansion can be calculated as
α = 0.0148
. This assumes a
∆ SOC
of 1 for 100 % state of charge.

At higher states of charge, the finite element model must be able to build prestresses through volume expansion. For the pouch cell simulations, the multi-step method based on the interface_springback_lsdyna keyword was used to initialize the model. Expansion in the cell is applied by setting the volume expansion coefficient and applying the desired SOC change (i.e., 1 for 100 % SOC). At the end of the simulation, nodal and element history variables such as stresses, plastic strains and updated node locations are recorded in the “dynain” file. In the subsequent simulation, the dynain file is imported to apply the prestresses and strains and update the node locations in the model before applying the mechanical load, i.e., cylindrical punch. This method was examined using the four cases (Case A-D) discussed in the previous section. In the finite element models of Case A and B, the volume expansion phase was modeled without any constraints on the cell. A rigid wall was placed under the battery, allowing it to expand freely in the thickness direction, as depicted in Fig. 8a. For Case C, the battery was confined in the thickness direction between two rigid walls before applying the volume expansion (Fig. 8b). For the battery in Case D, an additional step was introduced before volume expansion. A rigid plate was used to apply a tightening force of 5 kN on the surface of the cell (Fig. 8c). The dynain file from the first simulation was transferred to the subsequent simulation where volume expansion induced additional stresses in the model followed by punch loading in the third phase.

In the previous section, the details of the finite element model of the pouch and cylindrical batteries were discussed. It was also explained how to induce prestresses in the model using volume expansion to resemble the conditions of the specified state of charge. After reaching the assigned state of charge, the finite element models were subjected to mechanical loading and were subsequently validated against the experimental data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation results of the cylindrical Cell
ce:section.ce:para: For the cylindrical cell, simulation results at 0 % SOC were discussed thoroughly in (Gilaki et al.) and the model was validated using experimental data. At higher states of charge, since the hard casing prevented the free volume expansion of the jellyroll, stresses were developed during dynamic relaxation. Fig. 9a plots the distribution of radial stresses in the jellyroll at 100 % SOC before transient structural simulation began. The stresses were in the range of 1.23–1.26 MPa. The expansion of the jellyroll caused higher stresses in the casing where the areas in contact with the jellyroll experienced maximum stresses of about 47 MPa (see Fig. 9b). Although the stresses in the jellyroll seem insignificant (i.e., less than about 1.2 MPa), the presence of prestresses means that when further mechanical loading is applied, the initial conditions include nonzero stresses and strains. Starting from nonzero compressive strains leads to a stiffer response during mechanical loading.

After applying the volume expansion, the battery was crushed between two rigid plates (Fig. 9c). Based on the experiments, three SOC levels of 0 %, 50 % and 100 % were considered. The force-displacement curves obtained from the models were compared to the experimental results in Fig. 9d. A similar approach was followed for the rod indentation case (Fig. 9e) and the resulting force-displacement curves were compared for 0 % and 100 % SOC (Fig. 9f). To provide a numerical comparison between the finite element results and experiments, Table 3 lists and compares the displacements at 30 kN for flat compression and 6 kN for rod indentation cases. The force values were chosen to be about two-thirds of the maximum force at 0 % SOC so that the corresponding displacements could be used as a measure of the shift in the force-displacement curves at higher SOCs. A comparison of the displacement values reveals that, for the flat compression configuration, the FE models predict the experimental data with errors ranging from 0.88 % to 1.83 %. The error for the rod indentation is below 1 %. In both the transverse compression and rod indentation cases, the data from the FE models correspond well with the experiments, confirming that the method discussed works effectively for cylindrical batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation results of the pouch Cell
ce:section.ce:para: Before studying the effects of confinement and prestresses on the mechanical response of pouch cells, the punch loading scenario was modeled at 0 % SOC. Fig. 10 shows the punch battery model and the resulting force-displacement curve. Comparison with the curve found from the test shows that the finite element model accurately follows the experimental data at 0 % SOC thus validating the calibrated material and the developed FE model. The model also needed to be validated at 100 % SOC under various levels of confinement (Case A-D). The first case studied was the unconfined charged cell. This model was subjected to free expansion (Case A). After applying a SOC change of 100 %, a 0.1191 mm increase in the thickness was observed which showed less than 0.6 % error compared to the 0.1184 mm value found from the experiments (see Fig. 11a). Fig. 11b shows the stress distribution of the battery in the Z (thickness) direction. The stresses in the cell were in the order of 10−4 MPa, which is considered negligible. This was expected, considering the battery was unconfined and free to expand. The cell in Case B was also charged in a similar unconfined condition. For Case C, because of the restriction in the Z direction, stresses developed in the model as plotted in Fig. 11c. In this case, the stresses increased to about 0.177 MPa. For Case D, two stress contours are provided. Fig. 11d shows the stresses after the addition of the 5 kN force on the surface of the battery, which are in the range of 0.181–0.184 MPa. This is comparable to the theoretical stress calculated based on the 5 kN force applied on the surface of battery (210 mm × 130 mm) which is 0.183 MPa. The next plot (Fig. 11e) is for the same cell after volume expansion, which leads to higher stresses of about 0.422 MPa.

After transferring the prestresses from the dynain file, the punch loading was applied to the batteries in Cases A-D. Preloading conditions for Cases A and B were similar. Because the prestresses for both cases were negligible, the resulting punch force-displacement curves were almost identical, matching the results obtained from the experiments (see Fig. 11f). The results for Case C & D showed higher stiffness due to the added prestress values and they were also in a very good agreement with the experiments. Similar to the comparison provided for cylindrical cells, Table 4 lists the error in simulation results based on the indentation depths at 30 kN. Since the maximum force for cylindrical punch tests on pouch batteries and flat compression tests on cylindrical cells were identical, similar force of 30 kN was chosen to compare the displacements between the FE models and the experiments. The error for the indentation values, ranged between 0.19 % and 1.43 %. Overall, the results show that the finite element models can accurately predict the response of the pouch cell for all the cases, thus validating the proposed method of applying volume expansion to the battery.

In order for the FE model to accurately predict the battery’s behavior at different SOC levels, the following steps need to be performed:
•
Characterize the battery’s mechanical behavior at 0 % SOC under the given loading scenario.
•
Perform experiments to monitor and measure expansion in the jellyroll or electrode stacks during charging.
•
Estimate the free volume expansion coefficient based on the measured data.
•
Apply the volume change in the jellyroll using thermal expansion in the finite element model to induce preload in the battery.
•
Perform mechanical loading to the prestressed model, replicating the battery at higher state-of-charge conditions.
Battery characterization has been extensively studied in various publications including (Kermani and Sahraei, 2017). Alternative methods for the other steps are further discussed below.

In order to estimate the amount of thickness change in jellyroll layers during charge and discharge, a few methods can be used depending on the form factor of the battery. The most straightforward approach is to monitor the thickness of the electrode stack or jellyroll directly. Because of the soft enclosure, this method works well for pouch cells and it is not practical for cylindrical or prismatic cells unless a prototype with a soft casing can be fabricated by the manufacturer. Digital image correlation similar to the method explained in Li et al. (2018), laser sensors, LVDTs or any other accurate displacement measurement tools can be used for thickness measurement. Another approach is to CT scan the battery at discharged and fully charged states and estimate thickness changes of several jellyroll layers as demonstrated in the current study for cylindrical batteries. This method requires high-resolution CT data which leads to long scanning times and can be quite costly. Furthermore, if intermediate SOC levels are also desired for more accurate data, CT must be repeated at those states. There are other methods that can potentially work for cylindrical batteries. One approach, which has not been tested by the authors, is to attach strain gauges on the surface of the battery and measure circumferential casing strain during charge and discharge. Then, a numerical or analytical method needs to be applied to estimate the amount of expansion in the jellyroll causing the measured strain in the casing. Depending on the form factor of the battery and the availability of tests, any other approach that can lead to an accurate volume expansion coefficient in the jellyroll can be successfully used to apply the present method to lithium-ion batteries.

Applying the measured volume expansion to the finite element method can be performed using different techniques. The most stable method is using dynamic relaxation, as explained in Section 3.1 for cylindrical cells. The expansion is applied in a pseudo-time phase preceding the regular transient phase. During the preload, nodal velocities are continuously reduced by a dynamic-relaxation factor which has a damping effect. This minimizes the chances of instabilities in the model during the preload stage. The drawback of this approach is that if there are other sources of preload in addition to SOC, it would be best to apply them in separate steps to avoid numerical instabilities. The second approach is to apply the preload in a separate simulation. By activating the interface_springback_lsdyna keyword in LS-DYNA, initial stresses and strains are saved in a file named “dynain”, which can be imported into the subsequent simulation. The advantage of this method is that if the results of the preload are satisfactory, the preload stage does not need to be repeated for every rerun of the main transient simulation. This can be efficient when multiple mechanical load cases are studied for a given state of charge. It should be noted that volume expansion during cycling mostly occurs in the anode, which is graphite in most Li-ion cells. Therefore, this process is expected to be similar across different chemistries and form factors. The primary difference is the type of confinement in a jellyroll versus a stack, or the thickness and porosity of the coatings, which can cause different coefficients of expansion at the cell level for each specific cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications and limitations of the proposed approach
ce:section.ce:para: The main advantage of the method proposed in this study is its capability to develop FE models at higher SOC states using a base model developed at zero SOC. FE models are often calibrated using characterization tests conducted on discharged cells. However, in real-world battery design scenarios, CAE models are frequently used to replicate batteries under worst-case conditions, which typically occur at higher SOC levels. The proposed approach can be a powerful tool for adjusting FE models and efficiently predicting the battery's response at the desired state of charge. Another application of this approach could be modeling a battery module or pack consisting of hundreds or thousands of cells when the cells are imbalanced. The model can be easily initialized by applying different SOC levels to individual cells before running the main loading scenario.

It should be noted that one of the main assumptions in the previous sections was that volume expansion linearly changes with SOC. This assumption leads to a constant α in Eq. (1). However, to achieve maximum accuracy, a more refined characterization of volume expansion is also possible. In that case, a table of volume expansion coefficients corresponding to SOC steps between 0 % and 100 % can be used to initialize the FE model more accurately to the desired SOC. Another simplification of the proposed method is that volume expansion is applied uniformly to the jellyroll or electrodes stack. If electrodes swell more towards the center of the cell, this nonuniformity cannot be captured by the current model. Furthermore, all the volume expansion discussed and modeled using this approach is due to thickness change in electrodes. Preloads and thickness changes originating from excessive gas generation inside the cell were not part of this study. Additionally, we have not focused on modeling failure and short circuits at different SOC levels. This will be addressed in future studies.

A method was proposed to simulate the response of pouch and cylindrical cells at higher SOC levels from models created at discharged states. An efficient approach was used to estimate the amount of volume expansion in electrodes during charging and simulate the stiffening effects of the cells as SOC increases. By applying this method, the preload caused by volume expansion was implemented to create FE models equivalent to higher SOC states. Mechanical loads were applied to the 50 % and 100 % SOC models, and comparison with experiments confirmed that the method accurately predicts the behavior of lithium-ion batteries at higher SOC levels.

One of the advantages of the proposed approach is that it reduces the number of experiments required to fully characterize batteries at different states of charge. Additionally, this method works independently of the cell's form factor and level of confinement. It should be noted that the accuracy of the final model strongly depends on the method and quality of the test performed to find jellyroll’s volume expansion coefficient. Further research is needed to evaluate the relevance of this method to other form factors, such as prismatic cells.

Mehdi Gilaki: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Elham Sahraei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the funding from multiple sources for this research. This includes support of Temple University Startup funds, MIT Industrial battery consortium, Office of Naval Research Contract N000141912351 and N00014–23–1–2612. This research includes calculations carried out on HPC resources supported in part by the National Science Foundation through major research instrumentation grant number 1625061 and by the US Army Research Laboratory under contract number W911NF-16–2–0189.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Xu et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupling Effect of State-of-Health and State-of-Charge on the Mechanical Integrity of Lithium-Ion Batteries
Exp. Mech.

Sci. Rep.
